title: Integrated rate laws and reaction order
definition: An **integrated rate law** solves the rate law for concentration as a function of time — it turns a rate into a prediction. The form depends on the **order**: **zero order** gives $[\mathrm{A}] = [\mathrm{A}]_0 - kt$ (a straight line that reaches **zero** at a finite time, half-life $t_{1/2} = [\mathrm{A}]_0/2k$, which *shrinks*); **first order** gives $[\mathrm{A}] = [\mathrm{A}]_0 e^{-kt}$ (exponential decay, $t_{1/2} = \ln 2/k$, *constant* — the signature); **second order** gives $\frac{1}{[\mathrm{A}]} = \frac{1}{[\mathrm{A}]_0} + kt$ (asymptotic, $t_{1/2} = 1/k[\mathrm{A}]_0$, which *grows*). Each linearizes a different plot — $[\mathrm{A}]$, $\ln[\mathrm{A}]$, or $1/[\mathrm{A}]$ versus $t$ — which is how the order is read from data. The order is a disclosed model (regime-2) and $k$ is sourced (regime-3), but the law that follows is exact, so a **constant half-life is not a general fact — it is the fingerprint of first order** (see [[half-life]]).
